title: The Algebra of Compiler Passes: An Empirical Study of Idempotency, Commutativity, and Convergence in LLVM Optimization Pipelines
description: We systematically characterize the algebraic properties of LLVM compiler optimization passes -- idempotency, pairwise commutativity, and constructive/destructive interference -- across diverse benchmark programs. By treating passes as operators and measuring their compositional behavior, we build a pass interaction algebra that explains phenomena like optimization oscillation, reveals redundancies in standard pipelines, and enables a lightweight algebra-guided pass ordering heuristic competitive with existing methods.
motivation: The phase-ordering problem -- selecting and sequencing compiler optimization passes -- has been studied for decades using search-based methods (RL, genetic algorithms, random search). However, these methods treat passes as opaque black-box actions without exploiting their intrinsic mathematical properties. Practitioners have long observed that some passes are idempotent, some commute, and some destructively interfere, yet no systematic empirical characterization exists. Understanding these algebraic properties could fundamentally reduce the effective search space and provide principled foundations for pass ordering.
proposed_approach: We conduct a large-scale empirical study on ~50 LLVM optimization passes across 75+ benchmark programs (cBench, PolyBench, LLVM test-suite). We measure: (1) idempotency -- whether applying a pass twice equals applying it once; (2) pairwise commutativity -- whether pass order matters for all ~1,225 pairs; (3) interference -- whether combined pass effects are synergistic or destructive; (4) convergence -- how quickly iterative pipeline application reaches a fixed point; (5) cycle detection -- identification of minimal oscillating pass sequences. From these measurements, we construct a pass interaction algebra and derive a lightweight ordering heuristic that exploits synergistic relationships while avoiding destructive interference. All experiments run on CPU using LLVM's opt tool.

related_work.title: Mitigating the Compiler Optimization Phase-Ordering Problem using Machine Learning
related_work.relevance: Pioneering work formulating phase ordering as a Markov process. Treats passes as opaque actions; we characterize their algebraic properties.

related_work.title: MiCOMP: Mitigating the Compiler Phase-Ordering Problem Using Optimization Sub-Sequences and Machine Learning
related_work.authors: Amir H. Ashouri, Andrea Bignoli, Gianluca Palermo, Cristina Silvano, Sameer Kulkarni, John Cavazos
related_work.relevance: Decomposes pass sequences into sub-sequences. Our interference analysis provides theoretical grounding for why certain sub-sequences work.

related_work.title: CompilerDream: Learning a Compiler World Model for General Code Optimization
related_work.authors: Chaoyi Deng, Jialong Wu, Ningya Feng, Jianmin Wang, Mingsheng Long
related_work.relevance: State-of-the-art model-based RL for pass ordering. Learns a world model of the compiler; our algebraic characterization provides complementary analytical understanding.

related_work.title: Synergy-Guided Compiler Auto-Tuning of Nested LLVM Pass Pipelines
related_work.authors: Haolin Pan, Jinyuan Dong, Mingjie Xing, Yanjun Wu
related_work.relevance: Uses synergy knowledge graphs for pass ordering. Our interference quantification provides a formal foundation for their empirical synergy notion.

related_work.title: Efficient Compiler Optimization by Modeling Passes Dependence
related_work.authors: Yuanjie Liu, Jianfeng Fang, Wenming Wang, Hao Xue, Hanlin Huang, Jian Wang
related_work.relevance: Closest work: builds optimization dependence graphs. We go beyond sequential dependence to algebraic properties (commutativity, idempotency, interference magnitude).

related_work.title: POSET-RL: Phase Ordering for Optimizing Size and Execution Time using Reinforcement Learning
related_work.authors: Shalini Jain, Yashas Andaluri, S. VenkataKeerthy, Ramakrishna Upadrasta
related_work.relevance: Uses partially ordered sets for pass ordering. Our commutativity analysis provides the empirical foundation for which orderings are truly constrained.

related_work.title: CompilerGym: Robust, Performant Compiler Optimization Environments for AI Research
related_work.relevance: Provides the experimental infrastructure (environments, benchmarks) we build upon.

related_work.title: ProGraML: A Graph-based Program Representation for Data Flow Analysis and Compiler Optimizations
related_work.authors: Chris Cummins, Zacharias V. Fisches, Tal Ben-Nun, Torsten Hoefler, Michael F P O'Boyle, Hugh Leather
related_work.relevance: Graph-based program representation for ML-driven optimization. Complementary to our pass-centric analysis.

hypothesis: LLVM optimization passes exhibit rich, non-trivial algebraic structure: a significant fraction are empirically idempotent, commutativity is highly non-uniform across pass pairs, measurable constructive and destructive interference exists between passes, and this structure can be exploited to construct competitive optimization sequences with dramatically reduced search effort.
success_criteria: At least 60% of passes are strongly or weakly idempotent across the majority of benchmarks
At least 30% of pass pairs are non-commutative (demonstrating that order truly matters)
At least 10% of pass pairs exhibit significant constructive or destructive interference (>5% instruction count difference)
Oscillation is detected in at least some benchmarks under iterative pipeline application
The algebra-guided ordering achieves instruction count reduction within 5% of -O2 using fewer passes, or measurably improves upon -O2
Commutativity and interference structures reveal interpretable clusters aligned with pass categories
